item.title: We’ve Become Too Savage for ‘Lord of the Flies’
item.description: Savagery just ain’t what it used to be.

item.title: It’s Not a Mystery Why Fertility Rates Are Declining
item.description: More and more, smaller families and a more manageable work-life balance are the goal.

item.title: Trump Just Pardoned Himself and His Family Forever
item.description: By issuing a prohibition on federal investigations that might threaten Trump’s finances, the Justice Department has placed the president and his family in a new category.

item.title: Breaking the Two-Party System
item.description: America’s two-party system isn’t an accident — it is a direct result of our electoral structure. The political scientist Lee Drutman tells the Opinion columnist Ezra Klein how shifting to proportional representation could pave the way for viable third parties and give voters more choices.

item.title: Now Give Me Money (That’s What I Want)
item.description: Trump has molded the presidency in his image. It isn’t pretty.

item.title: Why Are Kids Using A.I. at School?
item.description: A.I. is replicating the pattern followed by every ed-tech quick fix.

item.title: What Silicon Valley Is Coming for Next
item.description: Hint: It comes from inside of you.

item.title: Trump, the Audits and the Payout Fund
item.description: Readers respond to news that the I.R.S. would be barred from pursuing tax claims against President Trump, his family or his businesses. Also: Protecting precious places; growing native plants.

item.title: I Found a College Where I Came First
item.description: Historically Black colleges and universities hold steady against the churn of political retreat and cultural amnesia.

item.title: E. Jean Carroll Taught Me to Not Be Afraid
item.description: It’s not easy to make an anti-Trump documentary right now.

item.title: Where Are the Republicans Who Put America First?
item.description: The “Trump First” wing of the G.O.P. is in ascendance. The rest of the party needs to fight back.

item.title: Trump’s Slush Fund Will Reward Criminals. Americans Will Pay for It.
item.description: The president’s corruption and subversion of democratic tradition risk becoming the norm.

item.title: One Chatbot to Rule Everything
item.description: A.I. is influencing everything, from what we wear to what we read. Now it’s coming for something more intimate: human taste. On “The Opinions,” the question is what happens when A.I. becomes the filter for how we experience culture.

item.title: Is There a Door No. 3 for Democrats?
item.description: Is there anything Democrats can do to break free of a deeply polarized political system in which parties are constantly winning and then losing office?

item.title: A Cattle Ranch Is Doing What Ivy League Colleges Can’t
item.description: College education does almost nothing to develop the sense that what we do in our day-to-day lives resonates with people beyond ourselves.

item.title: How I Lost a Religion
item.description: Lessons from sports fandom on why traditions fade.

item.title: Thank You, President Trump
item.description: The president’s trolling of Canada, Greenland and Venezuela over becoming America’s next state has had one positive side effect, the Opinion columnist Jamelle Bouie argues.

item.title: Hatred of Israel and the Degradation of the West
item.description: Feeding narratives to the credulous isn’t speaking truth to power.

item.title: Which Has Better Taste: A.I. or Humans?
item.description: Why Silicon Valley is obsessed with taste.

item.title: I’m the Foreign Minister of Sweden. Don’t Overestimate Russia.
item.description: We can’t afford to underestimate Russia’s strength and its ability to harm Ukraine and NATO allies, but we must not overestimate it either.

item.title: Is This the End of Close Elections?
item.description: Rampant gerrymandering and intense partisan sorting have shrunk the number of competitive House seats to a record-low. The political scientist Lee Drutman explains how on “The Ezra Klein Show.”

item.title: Kentucky Had an Easy Choice Between Massie and Trump
item.description: The president wants a loyal Republican Party — even if the country might not.

item.title: Lamenting the End of Colbert’s ‘Late Show’
item.description: Readers will miss Stephen Colbert’s “joy, wit, nonsense and sanity,” in the words of one writer. Also: President Trump’s fund; Kash Patel’s snorkeling trip; Meta’s retort.

item.title: This Redistricting Chaos Must End
item.description: First on Democrats’ to-do list must be banning gerrymandering, restoring voting rights protections and reforming the Supreme Court.

item.title: Trump Has Done More Than Misjudge Iran
item.description: The war has revealed the true puniness of Trump’s power

item.title: MAGA Women Are Leading a #Me2.0 in Washington
item.description: These G.O.P. crusaders might be the perfect people to fight sexual misconduct on Capitol Hill.

item.title: The Pressing Case for Supreme Court Reform
item.description: In a representative democracy, no one should hold so much power for so long.

item.title: Why College Grads Are Booing Their Commencement Speakers
item.description: The generation that grew up with A.I. hates it.

item.title: How to End the Gerrymandering Doom Loop Forever
item.description: The political scientist Lee Drutman argues that we should switch to a system of proportional representation and put an end to our “trench warfare politics.”

item.title: How to End the Gerrymandering Doom Loop Forever
item.description: The political scientist Lee Drutman argues that we should switch to a system of proportional representation and put an end to our “trench warfare politics.”

item.title: The End of Reality TV Politics
item.description: Reality TV politics is coming to an end because, Sarah Isgur argues, reality TV itself is dying. On “The Opinions,” she explains why she believes voters are now gravitating toward more optimistic, Ted Lasso-style candidates instead.

item.title: Demon Attacks. Teleportation. Astrology. The Supernatural Is Back.
item.description: One of the oldest and most durable features of human experience is re-emerging.

item.title: Stephen Colbert’s Late Show Cancellation and the Fall of CBS
item.description: In shutting down a 33-year late-night franchise, CBS is assenting to its own diminishment.

item.title: The Vanity and Inanity of Trump’s White House
item.description: If you’re not strutting, you’re not selling

item.title: We Need More ‘Rabid Partisans’ Like Shane Massey
item.description: Defeating a sclerotic rump of a party is no achievement. Instead, one-party rule enables corruption. It fosters stagnation.

item.title: Palestinian Accounts of Rape by Israelis
item.description: Readers respond to a column by Nicholas Kristof. Also: Imposing religion.

item.title: Firing Squads Expose the Brutality of the Death Penalty
item.description: America is returning to firing squad executions after failing to find a more humane way to carry out the death penalty.

item.title: What A.I. Kant Do
item.description: Could we English majors have a future after all?

item.title: Tech Workers Have Fears About A.I., Too. They Can Do Something About It.
item.description: Even the developers of A.I. want regulation. They’re starting to take action.

item.title: Nothing Beats Polarization Like Civics Education
item.description: The decline of civic education hit bottom about a decade ago and is at last on the rebound. This fact brings me hope.

item.title: Getting Jeff Bezos to the Opera
item.description: Box office is waning. Donors are fading. Opera needs new lifeblood. The answer lies with tech fortunes.

item.title: Here’s the Trade Deal That Trump and Xi Should Have Reached
item.description: For this to happen, however, both men would need personality transplants.

item.title: The Great Disruption: How A.I. Will Shape Our Future
item.description: Readers discuss ways in which artificial intelligence is reshaping our lives. Also: Frequent redistricting.

item.title: France Has a New Picture of the Holocaust
item.description: A new collection of propaganda photos shows the first roundup of Jews in Paris, in 1941.

item.title: What A.I. Did to My College Class
item.description: Lessons from the first graduating A.I. class.

item.title: The Law They Hate Was a High Point of Our History
item.description: The Voting Rights Act has more democratic legitimacy than the court that is trying to destroy it.

item.title: China’s Peak Is Now
item.description: Does Xi Jinping see the world this way?

item.title: The Great Political Realignment of 2026
item.description: The country seems to be tiring of the Trump era. Now what?
